vulnerability.title: Add nosuid Option to /dev/shm
vulnerability.description: The nosuid mount option can be used to prevent execution of setuid programs in /dev/shm. The SUID and SGID permissions should not be required in these world-writable directories. Add the nosuid option to the fourth column of /etc/fstab for the line which controls mounting of /dev/shm.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if ( ! ( { rpm --quiet -q kernel-core ;} && { rpm --quiet -q rpm-ostree ;} && { rpm --quiet -q bootc ;} && { ! rpm --quiet -q openshift-kubelet ;} && ([ -f /run/ostree-booted ] || [ -L /ostree ]) ) && ! ( [ -f /.dockerenv ] || [ -f /run/.containerenv ] ) ); then function perform_remediation { mount_point_match_regexp="$(printf "^[[:space:]]*[^#].*[[:space:]]%s[[:space:]]" /dev/shm)" # If the mount point is not in /etc/fstab, get previous mount options from /etc/mtab if ! grep -q "$mount_point_match_regexp" /etc/fstab; then # runtime opts without some automatic kernel/userspace-added defaults previous_mount_opts=$(grep "$mount_point_match_regexp" /etc/mtab | head -1 | awk '{print $4}' \ | sed -E "s/(rw|defaults|seclabel|nosuid)(,|$)//g;s

triage_decision.reason: Adding the nosuid option to the /dev/shm mount is a simple configuration edit that does not affect authentication, networking, or critical services. The change is low risk; the only possible issue is a malformed /etc/fstab line causing a boot problem, which can be mitigated by testing the mount command before reboot.
triage_decision.estimated_impact: filesystems affected; Malformed /etc/fstab entry could prevent boot; Applications relying on setuid binaries in /dev/shm may be affected

review_feedback: The plan correctly targets adding the nosuid option to /dev/shm, but it edits /etc/fstab with a complex inline sed/awk one‑liner and does not create a backup first. A syntax error or quoting mistake could corrupt the fstab entry, potentially preventing the filesystem from mounting correctly and causing boot or service issues. While the change itself is safe, the method introduces unnecessary risk, so it should be reviewed and simplified before execution.

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The plan correctly targets adding the nosuid option to the /dev/shm mount entry in /etc/fstab, which will remediate the vulnerability if applied. However, the suggested sed/awk one‑liner is overly complex and prone to quoting errors. A safer approach would back up /etc/fstab, use a clearer command (e.g., awk or a separate variable), ensure the option is not duplicated, and optionally remount the filesystem immediately. Adding a backup step and a remount command would make the remediation more robust.
concerns: The sed command embeds an awk call inside a substitution; this may fail due to quoting or shell evaluation issues.
No backup of /etc/fstab is made before editing, increasing risk of a broken fstab.
The plan does not include an immediate remount to apply the change without a reboot.
Potential duplication of "nosuid" if the option is already present.
suggested_improvements: Create a backup of /etc/fstab (e.g., cp /etc/fstab /etc/fstab.bak) before editing.

Use a simpler, well‑tested command such as: awk '{if($2=="/dev/shm" && $1!~/^#/){split($4,opt,",");if(!("nosuid" in opt)) $4=$4",nosuid"}}1' /etc/fstab > /etc/fstab.tmp && mv /etc/fstab.tmp /etc/fstab
After editing, run: mount -o remount,nosuid /dev/shm to apply the change without reboot.
Verify the fstab syntax with `fstab -f` or by testing the mount command before reboot.
